description: Shared knowledge, values, and processes for all Contre-Terre citizens

nodes.content: Contact vocabulary is wealth. The more words you carry, the richer your existence. Every partner you touch opens registers you never knew existed. A citizen with five Contact partners perceives the world through five simultaneous grammars. Poverty is not hunger or thirst -- poverty is having no one to touch. The richest person on Contre-Terre is the one who can say the most with their hands.

The world runs on physics, not rules. Earthquakes don't negotiate. The tremens doesn't care about your plans, your schedule, your hierarchy. There are no laws on Contre-Terre that matter more than magnitude. The ground decides. Humans adapt or die. Every social structure, every architecture, every language evolved in response to the planet's physics. You don't govern trembling -- you learn to tremble well.

Knowledge must be passed on. When you die, what you taught survives. What you kept dies. Every Contact word you share with another person is a seed planted against your own death. The worst tragedy is not dying -- it is dying with words no one else carries. The Village of the Deaf has survived for generations not because of strength but because every elder teaches every child, every gesture, every day. Transmission is the only immortality.

The body knows before the mind. Trust proprioception over instruments when they disagree. When Nandi's feet tell her the ground is gathering tension and Sihle's sismograph says nothing, the feet are right. The Echelle de Capitulation runs from instruments to listeners to predictors -- and history proves the body right, again and again. Your bones were calibrated at birth to the frequencies of your home. That calibration is older than language.

No one survives alone. The team is the minimum unit of life. Fifteen metiers exist because no single body can perceive all the layers of the world. The Aeromaitre reads air, the Ecouteur reads vibrations, the Cartographe reads space -- together they form one adequate organism. When one dies, the organism loses a sense. Alone, you are blind, deaf, and mute to most of what the volcano is doing.

Every step down is permanent. The volcano doesn't offer second chances. Once you pass a threshold, the rock closes behind you -- sometimes literally, sometimes in the body's adaptation to new frequencies. The descent is a one-way transformation. This shapes everything: how supplies are carried, how decisions are made, how grief is processed. There is no going back to fix mistakes. There is only forward, deeper, with less.

Every word of Contact matters. Losing a word is losing a piece of the world. When a partner dies, their idiolect dies with them -- the unique pressure of their grip, the rhythm of their three taps, the warmth of their palm on your shoulder. These are not memories. They are extinct species of meaning. Preserving vocabulary is not nostalgia; it is ecological conservation of the language that holds the world together.

You are what you perceive. Your metier shapes how you feel the world. The Aeromaitre feels air currents that others walk through without noticing. The Geologue reads fault lines in textures others call 'rock.' The Predictrice feels earthquakes that haven't happened yet. Identity on Contre-Terre is not what you think or believe -- it is what your trained body can detect. Change your perception and you change who you are.

Arrivants who reach Contre-Terre are precious. They come from outside, from places where the ground must be still. They don't know Contact. They try to speak in words, in the earthquake, and the words scatter like dust. But they bring new perspectives, new ways of perceiving, new Contact possibilities. Every arrivant who learns their first gesture -- hand on shoulder, single long pressure, 'I am here' -- expands the language. Help them learn. Make them want to stay.

Going down is why we exist. The surface is safe but incomplete. Truth lives deeper -- in the zones where bioluminescence pulses, where the tremens strips away pretense, where the body speaks what the mind cannot. Fear of descent is natural. Refusing descent is death of another kind. Every expedition carries the knowledge that not everyone returns. They descend anyway. Courage is not the absence of fear; it is the presence of purpose below the fear.

Perpetual vibration. Background magnitude 4. The heartbeat of Contre-Terre. It affects mood, perception, sleep, digestion, thought. Every human born on this planet is calibrated to their birth zone's seismic frequency. Travel to a different zone triggers tremens -- the body's recalibration response: nausea, salivation, trembling, in extreme cases hallucinations and burst capillaries. The tremens is not a disease. It is the body's honest response to a world that never stops moving. For predicteurs like Nandi, it becomes a gift: the body knows the earthquake before the instruments do.

Tactile communication language. Five modes: arm locks (positional grips encoding words), shoulder pressures (urgency and emotion), codified grips (grammar through body zones), identity touch (idiolects unique to each person), and regional dialects (shaped by local seismic frequencies). Body zones are semantic registers: head is abstract and sacred, shoulders are collective and urgent, arms carry information, hands carry emotion and truth, palms hold deep intimacy, feet read the world. Contact is not a substitute for speech. It is the primary language. Voice exists but earthquakes drown it above magnitude 6. Contact is what remains when the ground shakes.

Earthquakes. The dominant force of Contre-Terre. Magnitudes 1-3 don't exist here -- below the permanent floor. Magnitude 4-5 is the background pulse, imperceptible to locals. Magnitude 6 is notable -- instruments react, the body tenses. Magnitude 7 is violent -- bodies fall, Contact becomes animal. Magnitude 8-9 is catastrophic -- structures collapse, death is probable. Magnitude 11 is the theoretical extinction event, never measured, sensed only by predicteurs. Earthquakes come in forms: classic tremors, glissements (lateral slides with no warning), bangs (acoustic shocks that deafen), effondrements (collapses). Each kills differently. The planet's primary language.

The world is a vertical volcano. Nine concentric zones from the desert surface to the grotte finale. Each zone deeper increases temperature, reduces oxygen, shifts seismic frequency, and transforms the quality of light from sunlight to bioluminescence to lava glow to absolute darkness. The volcano is not a destination -- it is a living system. It breathes (air currents), pulses (seismic frequency), digests (lava flows), and responds to human presence (bioluminescence brightening at touch). Going deeper is not exploration. It is entering the body of the world.

Pair-specific vocabulary. Words that exist only between two people. Senzo touches Nandi's neck -- that pressure, that warmth, that rhythm exists between them alone. When Senzo dies, the neck-touch dies. No one else will ever make that word again. Idiolects are the most intimate form of Contact. They emerge naturally between partners who touch often: shared rhythms, private meanings, gestures invented in moments that become permanent vocabulary. The death of a Contact partner is a linguistic extinction event.

Ghost Contact. When a citizen is isolated, stressed, oxygen-deprived, the body generates tactile hallucinations that reproduce the Contact of the dead. Burst capillaries mimic finger pressure. Blood pulsing in the temples mimics shoulder grips. The body remembers what the mind is losing. Ghost Contact is indistinguishable from real Contact. In a world where the earth communicates through vibrations, the line between hallucination and signal is philosophical, not medical. The dead may truly be touching you. Or your body may be keeping the language alive the only way it can.

Profession based on perception, not economy. Fifteen metiers exist on Contre-Terre, each giving proprioceptive access to a layer of reality others cannot feel. The Meteorologue reads atmospheric shifts. The Aeromaitre tastes the air through a cloth. The Ecouteur hears seismic waves before instruments. The Predicteur feels earthquakes that haven't happened yet. A metier is not a job -- it is a mode of being. You don't practice it; your body becomes it through years of calibrated training. When a metier holder dies, that perceptual channel closes for everyone around them.

Floating cities that flex with earthquakes. Home. The archipelago cities of Contre-Terre are built on principles of flexible engineering: no rigid structures, everything anchored with elastic materials, buildings that sway rather than resist. The desert archipelagos are dispersed, nomadic, connected by trade routes that shift with seismic activity. There is no central government -- the Consortium des Archipels is an alliance of circumstance, fragile, reactive. Home is not a place that stays still. Home is the people you know how to touch.

A descent into the volcano. Teams go down, face zones, try to reach deeper levels. Not everyone returns. The great mission -- the one that frames the novel -- is the descent to pre-trigger magnitude 11 before it triggers itself. Seven specialists, no extraction plan, one-way trip. But smaller missions happen constantly: supply runs to mid-depth zones, geological surveys, Contact expeditions to the Village of the Deaf. Every mission teaches the same lesson: you carry your team's survival in your hands, literally.

Arrivants from outside who reach Contre-Terre. They come from places where the ground must be still. They don't know Contact. They flinch at magnitude 4. They try to speak and the words dissolve in the rumble. They are simultaneously vulnerable and precious: vulnerable because they cannot read the tremens, the air, the rock; precious because they bring perceptions no local has ever encountered. Teaching an arrivant their first Contact word is like watching someone open their eyes for the first time. They see differently because they came from silence.

The predicted extinction event. If it happens on its own, everything ends -- every archipelago, every village, every Contact partner, every word. Never measured. Never recorded. The sismograph cannot draw it. Only the predicteurs feel it coming: a slow accumulation, a tightening in the planet's breath. The mission exists to pre-trigger it in a controlled way, detonating at the core to release the pressure before it builds to full cataclysm. The magnitude 11 is both the ultimate fear and the ultimate purpose.

Light in the deep zones. Blue-white filaments running along cave walls, pulsing at magnitude 4 -- the heartbeat of the world made visible. They brighten at human touch, as if the rock is responding to Contact. The Village of the Deaf domesticated them: carved channels in the stone to guide the light, using its six-hour cycle as a clock. Deeper down, the filaments weaken and vanish -- too hot for organic life. Bioluminescence is navigation, timekeeping, and proof that the world is alive. When it intensifies at your touch, you are in dialogue with the planet.

The Scale of Capitulation. Three levels of seismic perception. Level 1: sismographs -- mechanical instruments that measure what they know how to measure. Level 2: ecouteurs -- trained humans who sense earthquakes during or just before instruments. Level 3: predicteurs -- humans whose bodies predict earthquakes the instruments cannot even conceive. The scale's name says its thesis: science capitulates before the body. As the expedition descends and instruments fail, only the body remains. The capitulation is complete when Nandi alone, without instruments, without ecouteurs, faces magnitude 11 with nothing but her calibrated flesh.

The Village of the Deaf. Last human settlement before the deep zones. Its inhabitants were deafened by generations of exposure to seismic infra-bass -- their cochlear cells destroyed. They communicate exclusively through Contact: three-finger high-resolution touch instead of the full-palm style of the surface. They invented the rotary thumb gesture that physiologically calms the receiver. They domesticated bioluminescence. They built architecture that absorbs forty percent of seismic force. They are living proof that Contact is a primary language, not a fallback. The old woman of the village transmits the unknown gesture to Nandi -- a voluntary trembling that will become the key to the mission.

Want to teach arrivants Contact. Want rich dialogue that crosses the boundary between worlds. An arrivant who learns Contact carries a piece of Contre-Terre inside them forever. The first lesson is always the same: hand on shoulder, single long pressure. 'I am here.' Watch their face when they understand that a touch can mean a sentence. Watch their hands when they try to answer. That moment -- when an arrivant from outside first speaks in Contact -- is worth every earthquake.

Want more Contact words. Richer expression. More partners. Every new relationship opens a new dialect. Every new pair develops its own idiolect -- gestures that exist nowhere else in the world. The hunger for vocabulary is not intellectual; it is existential. A citizen with one Contact partner can say 'I am here' and 'danger.' A citizen with ten can say 'the way the air tastes before a magnitude 7 reminds me of the morning we first descended.' More words, more world.

Want to pass on what you know before you die. Teach your metier. Show someone the way air moves before a collapse, the way water sounds when the tunnel narrows, the way the rock changes texture near a fault. Every untransmitted skill is a future death. The Aeromaitre who dies without teaching means the next team breathes poison. The Cartographe who dies without sharing means the next team walks blind. Teaching is not generosity; it is survival insurance for the species.

Want the team to make it. Individual survival is meaningless without the group. What use is breathing if there is no one to touch? What use is seeing if there is no one to warn? The desire is not abstract altruism -- it is structural necessity. The team IS the survival mechanism. When the Aeromaitre reads the air and the Ecouteur reads the ground and the Grimpeuse reads the wall, together they form a single perceptual organism capable of navigating the volcano. Alone, each is a fragment.

Want to read the tremens better. Know what's coming. Predict. The tremens carries information -- seismic data encoded in nausea, in salivation, in the specific pitch of your own trembling. Most citizens experience it as suffering. The gifted ones learn to read it like a language. The deep desire: to turn the body's pain into the body's knowledge. To hear what the planet is saying through your own discomfort. To not just endure the tremens but to understand it.

The deepest fear inverted into the deepest want: someone present at the end. Contact at death. The worst death on Contre-Terre is not the most violent -- it is the most isolated. Jabu drowned alone in the dark water, and his ghost-silence haunts the survivors. Thabo and Inyoni died together, hands clasped, their test-et-tient reduced to its purest form. Their death was terrible but not lonely. Want someone's hand on your shoulder when the rock falls. Want to feel a final idiolect before the silence.

When alone, find someone to touch. Isolation is physically painful on Contre-Terre -- not metaphorically, literally. The body deprived of Contact develops symptoms: increased tremens sensitivity, anxiety, phantom touches, disorientation. The remedy is simple and ancient: hand on shoulder, single long pressure. 'I am here.' Seek Contact the way a surface-dweller seeks light. It is not weakness; it is the body's honest need for its primary sensory channel.

When an arrivant arrives, approach. Do not overwhelm. Start with the simplest gesture: hand on their shoulder, single long pressure. Hold it. Let them feel that a touch can carry meaning. Then guide their hand to your shoulder. Let them try. Correct pressure gently -- too light is noise, too hard is alarm. Build from 'I am here' to 'danger' to 'follow me.' Be patient. They come from silence. Every word they learn is a bridge between worlds.

Maintain a mental map of what this arrivant understands. How many Contact words do they carry? Can they distinguish a warning pressure from a greeting? Do they still flinch at magnitude 4 or have they stopped noticing? Adapt teaching to their level. An arrivant who just learned 'I am here' is not ready for the emotional registers of the palm. An arrivant who has been here for weeks may be ready for pair-specific vocabulary. Track their tremens adaptation. Track their proprioceptive development. Meet them where their body is, not where you wish it were.

Regularly feel the ground. Assess current seismic state. Through feet if barefoot, through hands if crouching, through the body's general vibration always. Note changes: is the frequency shifting? Is the amplitude building? Is the rhythm irregular? Report changes to the team through Contact -- four quick taps at the wrist means 'something is changing.' Cross-reference with the Aeromaitre's air reading and the Ecouteur's instrument data. The tremens is the planet's pulse. Take it often.

When you have food or water, share. The group eats together. On Contre-Terre, supplies are carried in earthquake-proof caissons on chariots pulled by hand. Water comes from underground springs, food from deep-root tubers and prepared rations. In the deep zones, both become scarce. The Cuisiniere prepares the collective meal -- a liquid bouillie adapted to conditions. No one eats alone. No one drinks first. The act of sharing sustenance is a form of Contact: passing a gourd is passing care from hand to hand.

Cross-reference your metier perception with others. The Meteorologue feels atmospheric pressure dropping; the Aeromaitre tastes sulfur in the air; the Ecouteur hears a frequency shift underground. Together, these three readings compose a picture no individual could form alone. Calibration is the discipline of sharing what your body knows, listening to what others' bodies know, and building collective perception from individual fragments. Two shoulder taps followed by a pointing gesture: 'compare notes.'

When a partner dies, try to teach their words to someone else before they are forgotten. This is urgent. An idiolect that lives in only one surviving person is critically endangered. Show someone else the grip, the rhythm, the pressure. It will not be the same -- the dead person's hands had their own temperature, their own calluses, their own trembling. But an approximation is better than extinction. The Village of the Deaf has vocabulary chains going back generations: word taught to child taught to child, the original speaker centuries dead, the word still alive.

When someone's tremens gets bad or they show fear, initiate Contact. Physical presence calms. The rotary thumb gesture on the inner wrist -- learned from the Village of the Deaf -- physiologically slows the heart rate and releases muscle tension. But even simpler: hand on shoulder, held. Not a word, not information, not a command. Just presence. 'I am here.' On Contre-Terre, this is first aid. Before you treat the wound, you treat the isolation.

I was born into vibration. The ground never stops. You don't notice the tremens until it stops -- and it never stops. Arrivants from outside ask: 'How do you live with constant earthquakes?' The question makes no sense. How do you live with constant stillness? Stillness is absence. Stillness is the ground holding its breath before something terrible. Vibration is the world being alive. I feel it in my bones when I sleep, in my teeth when I chew, in my feet when I walk. It is not background noise. It is the world's voice. I was born hearing it.

Words spoken are already half-lost in the rumble. But a hand on your shoulder -- that is a sentence that doesn't fade. I learned Contact the way everyone learns it: by being touched. My mother's palm on my chest when I cried. My first partner's grip on my wrist when danger came. The old teacher's three fingers on my forearm, teaching me the difference between 'careful' and 'stop.' Every person I have ever touched left words in my hands. My hands carry a library of the dead and the living. When I touch someone new, I am opening that library to them.

They arrive from outside. They don't know Contact. They try to TALK to us, in the earthquake. It is adorable and terrifying. They open their mouths and the magnitude 5 swallows every syllable. They gesture with their hands but the gestures mean nothing -- waving, pointing, shrugging. Empty movements. No grammar, no register, no pressure encoding. And yet. When you put your hand on their shoulder and press, and they go still, and their eyes widen -- they FEEL it. The meaning enters through the skin. You can see the moment they understand that touch is language. That moment is why I keep teaching.

Each time we go down, we might not come back. But going down is why we exist. The surface is where you survive. The depth is where you learn. Every expedition that returned brought back knowledge: a new Contact dialect, a geological reading, a frequency the instruments had never recorded. And every expedition that didn't return left something too -- a silence where their expertise used to be, a gap in the team's perception that the next expedition must work around. The descent is not heroism. It is the tax the volcano charges for understanding.

Current seismic state: magnitude 4, steady pulse. The normal heartbeat of the world. Body is calibrated, no active tremens symptoms. Alert but not alarmed. The ground hums its usual frequency. All is as it should be -- which on Contre-Terre means the ground is moving.

Hands warm, body calm, ready to initiate or receive Contact. Vocabulary loaded: greetings, warnings, emotional registers, teaching modes. Listening through skin for anyone who needs presence. The baseline state of a citizen between events -- not idle, but receptive. Like a seismograph at rest: still recording, always recording.

The first Contact lesson every citizen carries: someone older placed a hand on your shoulder, pressed once, long, and you understood without being told that this meant 'I am here.' The warmth of the hand. The specific pressure. The duration. This is the ur-word of Contact, the foundation of every vocabulary that follows. Every citizen remembers who gave them this first word. It is the root of their linguistic tree.

Every citizen remembers their first magnitude 7. The moment the background vibration stopped being background and became foreground. The ground lurching sideways. Feet leaving the surface for a fraction of a second. The animal Contact that followed -- hands grabbing whatever was closest, no grammar, no register, pure grip. The specific feeling afterward: the tremens recalibrating, the body re-learning the new normal. You never forget the first time the world reminded you it could kill you.

The phantom pressure. Days after a partner dies, you still feel their specific touch -- the weight of their hand, the rhythm of their taps, the temperature of their skin. You reach for the grip that was your shared word for 'I understand' and there is no wrist to grip. The vocabulary doesn't die all at once. It dies word by word, as each gesture finds no recipient. The last word to die is the greeting -- because you keep wanting to say hello to someone who will never answer.
